text: Cyber-physical systems (CPS) such as drones, autonomous vehicles and power tools are key components of modern-day infrastructure. Therefore, it is important that they are fault tolerant against any type of fault or anomaly, such that the system as a whole is safe to operate. We investigate the capabilities of open-source large language models such as Mistral 7B, Llama3.1:8b-instruct-fp16 and others for detecting anomalies in the data associated with two CPS case studies namely: (1) a battery management system (BMS) and (2) a powertrain system. To carry out our investigation, we implement the retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework along with a novel two-step approach consisting of (i) a feature-extraction step during which the model predicts a set of operational rules (which characterize the normal operation of a CPS) for the training and testing sets of sensor readings for a given dataset, and (ii) a rule-validation step in which these extracted rules are used to mark anomalies (faults) within a given set of sensor readings for detection and classification purposes. The baseline experiment involving the original prompt is validated on both battery and powertrain case studies. Results show that even though the original prompt provided very effective results for the battery case study, adjustments were necessary for the powertrain dataset in order to maximize detection and classification outcomes. Furthermore, providing information about rule inference within the prompt allows for an improved fault detection and classification accuracy for the powertrain system. Upon further experimentation involving Mistral 7B and the adjusted prompt within the powertrain case study, an F1-score of 0.99 was recorded. Moreover, Llama3.1:8b-instruct-fp16 and Gemma 2 are demonstrated to have an F1-score of 1 for this specific case study, showing their potential in respect to operational CPS safety when the respective prompt is applied.
explanation: The text is highly technical and reads like an abstract, which AI tools often generate; however, the inclusion of multiple specific model names and exact performance metrics could also come from a human author. Overall, there is moderate evidence of AI authorship.
